nativeRange.habitat: Found on shallow tropical coral reefs, typically perching on or near coral heads where it performs its cleaning behaviour.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical western Atlantic coral reefs, typically at 1-40 m depth.

diet.notes: The Neon Goby generally accepts a wide range of prepared and frozen foods in captivity, taking frozen mysis shrimp and brine shrimp readily, along with sinking pellets and frozen carnivore preparations. In the wild, individuals also pick external parasites from the bodies of larger reef fish at established cleaning stations.

avoidWith: conspecifics other than a mated pair

imageCandidates.notes: Nick Hobgood; 1621x2159. Tack-sharp full-body in-situ shot on hard coral; vivid iridescent electric-blue stripe on near-black body clearly shown. Best diagnostic pick.
Nick Hobgood; 2400x1800. Sharp full-body in-situ shot on great star coral, showing the fish's cleaning-station perching behaviour and the blue stripe; different coral/angle for variety.
Jmk7; 4288x2848. Vivid close-up with the electric-blue stripe strongly saturated; front/head crisp, rear body partly obscured behind coral so kept as non-recommended extra.

summary: The Neon Goby is a small Caribbean cleaner fish that perches on coral heads and picks parasites from larger reef fish. Generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly, it tends to do well kept singly or as a mated pair in a reef aquarium of at least 75 litres.
careNotes: The Neon Goby (Elacatinus oceanops) is recognised by a vivid iridescent blue stripe running along its near-black body, and is one of the classic cleaner gobies of the Caribbean. In the wild, individuals set up cleaning stations on coral heads and service groupers, snappers, and other reef fish by picking external parasites from their bodies. In the aquarium, the species is generally considered hardy and reef-safe, and has been reported to ignore corals and ornamental invertebrates. It is best kept singly or as a bonded pair, as unrelated individuals tend to fight. Captive spawning has been reported, with eggs laid in crevices or empty shells. Lifespan in captivity tends to be short — around 1 to 2 years — though lifespans approaching 3 years have occasionally been noted.
breedingNotes: The Neon Goby has been bred successfully in home aquariums. Pairs are monogamous and tend to spawn on the undersides of overhangs, in crevices, or inside empty shells, with the male typically guarding the eggs.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: tank size, water parameters, temperament, diet, reef-safe status.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy, native range, maximum length (5.0 cm TL), preferred temperature range (24.5-28°C, observed 21-28°C), monogamous mating, cleaner-fish ecology. Used to average the temperature range with LiveAquaria's 22.2-25.6°C.

fish.breedingNotes: Forms monogamous pairs and spawns in crevices or shells. Raising the larvae is generally considered the main challenge in captive breeding.
